The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.

mines.name: Galena Mine (OR)
mines.description: A traveling assayer's offhand remark about the silver content of rocks near a mountain spring set off a staking rush in 1878 that placed the Galena Mine (OR) at its center. The silver occurred chiefly as argentite and native silver in a carbonate gangue, making hand-sorting practical and giving high-grade ore shipments an attractive economic profile. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. The post office, which served the camp and surrounding district for three decades, was the connection to the wider world; its records give historians one of their most reliable windows into the composition of the workforce. A stock promotion scandal in the mine's middle years drove out the original investors and brought in new management, which proved to be an inadvertent improvement: the new superintendent was a better practical miner than his predecessor. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.

mines.name: Cable Cove Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Cable Cove Mine (OR) opened in 1874 after a grizzled veteran spotted the telltale iron-stained gossan capping a rich lode — the kind of surface sign that experienced eyes never miss. High-grade pockets produced spectacular specimens — wire gold, leaf gold, and crystalline masses — some of which were sold directly to collectors at a premium over bullion value. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. An early-morning powder explosion at the collar of the shaft leveled the headframe and blew the hoist room off its foundation, fortunately occurring during shift change when the shaft was clear of men. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.

mines.name: Cornucopia Mine No. 2 (OR)
mines.description: The Cornucopia Mine No. 2 (OR) began as a placer strike in 1890: a forty-niner scooping creek gravel noticed colors rich enough to warrant hard-rock follow-up, and within months a shaft was sinking. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The superintendent's careful attention to ground support — regular inspection of timbering, prompt repair of dangerous sections — gave the mine an unusually good safety record for its era. Women's contributions to the camp economy were substantial and often overlooked; they ran boarding houses, laundries, and small stores that collectively processed more money than the mine itself in some lean years. Two parallel veins converged at depth into a single rich structure, a geological geometry that the mine's resident engineer had predicted on theoretical grounds but proved only after years of systematic development. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.

mines.name: Pine Valley Mine (OR)
mines.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1893, the Pine Valley Mine (OR) differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. Country rock alterations — pervasive silicification, adularia, and bladed calcite — gave the underground workings a distinctive appearance and confirmed the high-temperature hydrothermal origin of the ore. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.

mines.name: Rock Creek Mine (Baker OR)
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1863 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Rock Creek Mine (Baker OR) and begun sinking. The ore was refractory below the oxidized zone, requiring the installation of a cyanide plant in later years to recover gold that the stamps and amalgam plates could not capture. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. Silicification of the wallrock created a hard, persistent hanging wall that minimized dilution and reduced the need for extensive timbering in the upper levels. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.

mines.name: Powder River Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Powder River Mine (OR) was incorporated in 1862 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. Underground conditions were hard: summer heat in the lower levels, constant moisture, the acrid smell of blasting powder, and ever-present silica dust that shortened miners' lives and left many in later years disabled by silicosis. During a record snowfall winter, supply wagons could not reach the mine for six weeks, and the crew worked on reduced rations until the road was reopened — a testament to the determination of the men and their families. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. A local historical society has stabilized the surviving headframe and ore bins, and occasional guided tours bring a trickle of visitors who connect the industrial ruin to the period photographs on display inside. Metal detecting is permitted on this BLM property for recreational prospectors; collection of common mineral specimens is allowed, but removing paleontological or significant cultural artifacts requires prior authorization.

mines.name: Auburn Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Auburn Mine (OR) was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1861 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The company maintained a library of technical and general reading for its employees — an early example of the paternalistic amenities that larger mining corporations were beginning to offer in the reform era. Two parallel veins converged at depth into a single rich structure, a geological geometry that the mine's resident engineer had predicted on theoretical grounds but proved only after years of systematic development. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Years of weathering and vandalism have reduced the surface structures to their essence, but the mine remains a recognizable industrial landscape rather than just a scar on the hillside. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.

mines.name: Bohemia Mine No. 2 (OR)
mines.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1894, the Bohemia Mine No. 2 (OR) came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. The mine employed a physician during peak operations, a relatively unusual luxury that spoke to the company's interest in keeping its skilled workforce healthy and on-site. Smallpox swept the camp one winter, closing the boarding houses and forcing the temporary suspension of production until the epidemic passed and the workforce could safely reassemble. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The site is accessible via a graded dirt road maintained by the county, and the surviving structures are visible from the road without the need to enter the fenced hazard zone around the shaft collar. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.

mines.name: Noonday Mine (OR)
mines.description: A chance glint of free gold in the tailings of an abandoned hydraulic pit led to the founding of the Noonday Mine (OR) in 1898, launching what would become one of the district's longer-lived operations. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. During a record snowfall winter, supply wagons could not reach the mine for six weeks, and the crew worked on reduced rations until the road was reopened — a testament to the determination of the men and their families. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The camp once bustled with the noise of stamps, drills, and hoists; today it is silent except for the wind, the old camp roads slowly disappearing under encroaching vegetation. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.

mines.name: Vesper Mine (OR)
mines.description: An experienced drift miner who had spent fifteen years in the California camps identified the ore structure at this site in 1896 and organized the Vesper Mine (OR) with a handful of trusted partners. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. A two-room schoolhouse built by the mining company's wives' auxiliary educated the district's children through all twelve grades at its peak enrollment. A labor walkout in the mine's peak year idled the property for nearly three months, during which the owners brought in outside contractors who struggled to maintain the complex underground water balance. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.

mines.name: Lucky Boy Mine (OR)
mines.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1900, the Lucky Boy Mine (OR) came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. The company maintained an on-site blacksmith who sharpened drill steel daily, a vital operation in an era when hand-steel consumed more than it consumed drill bits. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. A runaway ore train on the inclined haulage wrecked the ore bin and damaged the crusher, shutting down production for three weeks while repairs were made and the haulage brake system redesigned. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.

mines.name: Blue Star Mine (OR)
mines.description: Cornish miners who had drifted north from the California camps staked the Blue Star Mine (OR) in 1902, bringing their tradition of deep, systematic hard-rock work to what had been a surface-only district. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. A dry house heated by a wood stove allowed miners to dry their wet clothing between shifts, reducing the frostbite and respiratory illnesses that plagued poorly managed camps. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. A catastrophic underground fire in the upper stopes destroyed several hundred feet of timber sets and forced a three-month shutdown while the burned-out sections were rehabilitated. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Interpretive signs at the trailhead describe the mine's history for visitors exploring the surrounding public land; the actual mine site is a short walk from the parking area. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.

mines.name: Myrtle Creek Mine No. 2 (OR)
mines.description: Brothers who had worked the Comstock bonanzas for a decade brought their savings and their skills to this district in 1858 and founded the Myrtle Creek Mine No. 2 (OR) on a vein they traced across three separate outcroppings. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. A two-room schoolhouse built by the mining company's wives' auxiliary educated the district's children through all twelve grades at its peak enrollment. Two parallel veins converged at depth into a single rich structure, a geological geometry that the mine's resident engineer had predicted on theoretical grounds but proved only after years of systematic development. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.

mines.name: Cow Creek Mine (OR)
mines.description: An experienced drift miner who had spent fifteen years in the California camps identified the ore structure at this site in 1857 and organized the Cow Creek Mine (OR) with a handful of trusted partners. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. The mine's payroll was the economic engine of the surrounding county, supporting farms, freighters, and merchants who collectively formed a community larger than the mine alone could explain. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. County historical records and a collection of mine logbooks preserved at the state historical society give researchers unusually detailed information about this property's operational history. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: Quines Creek Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Quines Creek Mine (OR) opened in 1856 after a grizzled veteran spotted the telltale iron-stained gossan capping a rich lode — the kind of surface sign that experienced eyes never miss. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Remnants of the surface plant — ore bins, sorting house, blacksmith shop — survive in various states of decay, representing one of the better-preserved examples of late-Victorian hard-rock mining infrastructure in the region. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.

mines.name: Wolf Creek Mine (OR)
mines.description: Winter did not stop the founding of the Wolf Creek Mine (OR); in 1855, men working frozen ground with picks and bars broke into a gold-bearing structure that would define the next two decades. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Tent cities gave way to frame structures within the first year, and within three years the camp had taken on the permanence of a real town, complete with a debating society and a brass band. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.

mines.name: Grave Creek Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Grave Creek Mine (OR) was staked in 1853 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. A union organizer arrived in the spring of the mine's busiest year and spent months carefully building a membership base before calling the strike that ultimately improved wage rates across the whole district. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The site draws rockhounds and history buffs; the tailings still yield fragments of the original ore, and the preserved headframe serves as a landmark visible for miles. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: Merlin Mine (OR)
mines.description: When the placer gold played out upstream in 1854, savvy prospectors followed the eroded channel to its source and established the Merlin Mine (OR) on the bedrock reef. The ore body proved wider at depth, encouraging the operators to push development rather than extraction and build up a substantial reserve of blocked ore. Miners worked by candlelight in the early years, later upgraded to oil-wick lamps, and eventually to carbide cap lamps that dramatically improved visibility and working conditions underground. The mine's ore reserve was carefully blocked out level by level; the systematic approach — unusual for its era — allowed management to plan production and investment on a multi-year horizon. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: Almeda Mine (OR)
mines.description: Gold fever brought Almeda Mine (OR) to life in 1898, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. The company's annual reports to shareholders — several of which survive in state archives — provide unusually detailed production and cost data for a mine of this size and era. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. The nearby town that developed to service the mine's workforce was a rough-edged but functional community, its main street lined with frame buildings that housed every service a working miner's paycheck could buy. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. A state historic marker at the road junction explains the mine's significance; visitors who venture up the old access road find the ruins more extensive than the marker suggests. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: Takima Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Takima Mine (OR) dates to 1900, when surface copper carbonates — bright green and unmistakable against the gray country rock — led a prospecting party to stake claims on what proved to be a substantial porphyry deposit. Native copper appeared sporadically in the upper workings — not enough to transform the economics, but enough to sustain early investor enthusiasm through the lean development years. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. The ore body's dip carried it under a neighboring claim, creating the legal disputes over apex rights that were common in this era and that eventually required a settlement negotiated by outside attorneys. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The surface plant has mostly vanished, but the mine's legacy persists in county records, family histories, and the occasional specimen jar of ore that turns up at a local antique fair. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: North Fork Myrtle Creek Mine (OR)
mines.description: The North Fork Myrtle Creek Mine (OR) began as a placer strike in 1860: a forty-niner scooping creek gravel noticed colors rich enough to warrant hard-rock follow-up, and within months a shaft was sinking. Arsenopyrite in the sulfide zone was both a nuisance and a marker: where it was densest, the gold values were highest, and the miners learned to follow the arsenic indicator. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. The skip could hoist several tons of broken ore per trip, and careful scheduling of hoisting and man-riding cycles kept the shaft operating efficiently through the long days. The nearby town that developed to service the mine's workforce was a rough-edged but functional community, its main street lined with frame buildings that housed every service a working miner's paycheck could buy. A runaway ore train on the inclined haulage wrecked the ore bin and damaged the crusher, shutting down production for three weeks while repairs were made and the haulage brake system redesigned. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The site is accessible via a graded dirt road maintained by the county, and the surviving structures are visible from the road without the need to enter the fenced hazard zone around the shaft collar. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: Ashland Mine (OR)
mines.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1854 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the Ashland Mine (OR) before the week was out. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Today the old mine yard is quiet, the headframe long since dismantled for salvage, but the outline of the ore bins and the concrete footings of the hoist room are still legible to a practiced eye. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.

mines.name: Griffin Creek Mine (OR)
mines.description: A party of Welsh hard-rock miners who arrived in 1854 recognized the geology at once — the pyritized schist, the carbonate alteration — and staked the Griffin Creek Mine (OR) before the week was out. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. The mine superintendent's house — larger and better furnished than the miners' cabins — stood on a slight rise above the camp, visible evidence of the social hierarchy that organized mining communities. Metamorphic aureole rocks around the intrusive complex provided the chemical trap for ore deposition, and the mine's geology bore a close resemblance to larger deposits in more famous districts. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.

mines.name: Trail Mine (OR)
mines.description: When prospectors traced a gold-bearing quartz reef up the canyon wall in 1856, the Trail Mine (OR) came into existence almost overnight, drawing capital and labor from across the territory. Telluride minerals — calaverite and sylvanite — appeared at depth alongside the free-gold ore, and their assay values sometimes exceeded the free-milling upper levels. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. Miners were paid by the contract system in the early years, each man responsible for a set amount of advance per shift; the system rewarded skill and speed but created fierce competition and occasional shortcuts on safety. A winter avalanche destroyed the lower section of the aerial tramway and isolated the mine from its mill for seven weeks; the company used the forced downtime to rehabilitate the underground ventilation system. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. The property is now managed as a recreational area; the underground workings are permanently sealed, but surface roads, tailings, and foundation outlines are accessible to visitors. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest

mines.name: Woodville Mine (OR)
mines.description: Backed by modest Eastern capital and big local ambition, the Woodville Mine (OR) sank its first shaft in 1858 and quickly proved that the quartz veins of this district ran deeper and richer than surface sampling had suggested. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. The mine supported a boarding house, a company store, and a small reading room — the latter a modest concession to reform-era pressure for better living conditions in isolated mining camps. Flooding in the lower workings following an unusually wet spring required emergency pumping operations that ran for six weeks before the water level was brought back under control. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Little remains above ground: the concrete foundation of the shaft house, the long gray ribbon of tailings, and a few ore-bin timbers slowly returning to the soil. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: Prospect Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Prospect Mine (OR) was incorporated in 1862 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. The district's geology was more complex than the simple bonanza vein that early promoters had advertised; faults offset the ore body repeatedly, requiring careful surveying and systematic exploration to maintain continuity. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The surface plant has mostly vanished, but the mine's legacy persists in county records, family histories, and the occasional specimen jar of ore that turns up at a local antique fair. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Rogue River-Siskiyou National Forest

mines.name: Drew Mine (OR)
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1865 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Drew Mine (OR) and begun sinking. The principal ore mineral was free-milling gold locked in quartz, simple enough to concentrate in a stamp mill and amalgamate on copper plates without expensive chemical treatment. Shifts ran around the clock during peak years, the mine never sleeping as the demand for ore outpaced the mill's capacity to process it. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.

mines.name: Tiller Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Tiller Mine (OR) was organized in 1867 after the district's assayer confirmed gold values that made even the most skeptical investors reach for their checkbooks. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. The vein system was part of a larger epithermal gold-silver network that geologists now understand as a single hydrothermal cell; at the time, miners just followed the ore wherever it led. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.

mines.name: Canton Mine (OR)
mines.description: The Canton Mine (OR) was incorporated in 1868 on ground that Indian and Mexican miners had worked by hand for years; the new company brought steam power and ambition to what had been a subsistence operation. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. A Methodist church built by mine families in the camp's early years provided a counterpoint to the saloon culture, and its congregation was a force for stability in an otherwise transient population. A stock promotion scandal in the mine's middle years drove out the original investors and brought in new management, which proved to be an inadvertent improvement: the new superintendent was a better practical miner than his predecessor. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. The stone assay office walls remain standing — thick enough to withstand a century of weather and neglect — while the surrounding wooden structures have long since collapsed. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.

mines.name: Canyonville Mine (OR)
mines.description: Gold fever brought Canyonville Mine (OR) to life in 1860, when prospectors following a free-gold float trace up a creek drainage broke into a promising quartz vein and staked the first claims. The oxidized zone proved exceptionally rich, but the transition to primary sulfide ore at the water table required the company to redesign its milling circuit at significant cost. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. The workforce was drawn from across the mining West: Cornish 'Cousin Jacks' ran the drilling crews, Irish muckers kept the stopes clean, Scandinavians handled the timber work, and Chinese laborers managed the most dangerous surface jobs. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Canyonville Mine (OR) site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.

mines.name: Riddle Mine (OR)
mines.description: Brothers who had worked the Comstock bonanzas for a decade brought their savings and their skills to this district in 1856 and founded the Riddle Mine (OR) on a vein they traced across three separate outcroppings. Gold-bearing quartz formed irregular shoots along the vein, so production was uneven — boom months of high-grade ore alternated with lean stretches of development work in barren ground. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. The mine's fatality rate was higher than average in its early years, reflecting both the dangerous ore body and management's tendency to prioritize production over safety improvements. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The old camp site and mine workings have slowly been absorbed into the surrounding landscape, but careful observers can still find the collapsed ruins of the ore bins and the distinctive gray-white tailings. Visitors may use metal detectors on this Bureau of Land Management site under casual-use provisions, making it a popular destination for weekend hobbyists and recreational gold prospectors.

mines.name: Glendale Mine (OR)
mines.description: In 1854, a surveying crew camping on the ridge noticed yellow flecks in their fire ring stones, and the Glendale Mine (OR) was born from that accidental discovery. The main vein struck a parallel structure at depth, effectively doubling the known ore reserve and justifying a major capital reinvestment in hoisting and milling equipment. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. A spectacular ore pocket discovered on the seventh level — massive, almost pure native metal — funded a complete renovation of the mill and hoist plant that set the mine up for another decade of profitable operation. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Today the old mine yard is quiet, the headframe long since dismantled for salvage, but the outline of the ore bins and the concrete footings of the hoist room are still legible to a practiced eye. As BLM land, metal detecting is allowed for recreational purposes under standard public land rules — avoid digging within 100 feet of visible historic structures and follow leave-no-trace principles.

mines.name: Pewabic Iron Mine (Gogebic)
mines.description: The Pewabic Iron Mine (Gogebic) was staked in 1886 on ground that earlier prospectors had dismissed as unpromising, but deeper testing revealed an ore body of real substance beneath the barren cap rock. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. Decline production in the mid-years prompted exploration crosscuts on the upper and lower levels, and the resulting ore discoveries extended the mine's productive life by a full decade. The nearby town that developed to service the mine's workforce was a rough-edged but functional community, its main street lined with frame buildings that housed every service a working miner's paycheck could buy. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. A state historic marker at the road junction explains the mine's significance; visitors who venture up the old access road find the ruins more extensive than the marker suggests. Hobbyists should contact the National Forest ranger district in advance — metal detecting requires a special use permit on USFS land.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Brotherton Mine (Gogebic)
mines.description: An independent prospector who had spent years studying the district's geology before staking a single claim founded the Brotherton Mine (Gogebic) in 1887. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. Immigrant miners from Bohemia, Scotland, Wales, and Cornwall brought specific craft knowledge — the Welsh for their understanding of timbering, the Cornish for deep-shaft methods — that shaped the mine's technical character. The deposit was zoned: near-surface gold grading into silver-dominant ore at depth, with lead-zinc values increasing in the lowest levels — a telescoped epithermal system that geologists found instructive. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Surface structures were demolished for salvage within a decade of closure, leaving only the tailings, a few stone walls, and the collapsed collar of the main shaft to mark the location. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Yale Mine (Gogebic)
mines.description: The Yale Mine (Gogebic) was organized in 1886 on promising ore that a government survey crew had noted but not pursued, leaving the discovery for private enterprise to develop. Drifts on the third and fourth levels followed the ore body east for several hundred feet before the vein pinched, then recovered with even better values in a new ore shoot two hundred feet further. Timber from nearby forests supplied the mine's insatiable need for set timbers, lagging, and fuel; the surrounding hillsides were largely stripped within a decade of the mine's founding. Crosscut tunnels from the main shaft sampled adjacent ground and in two cases discovered entirely new ore structures that extended the mine property and required additional surface claims. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. An unusually rich ore chimney was discovered when development drifts on two adjacent levels connected unexpectedly through the ore body itself, revealing a vertical pipe of native metal that astonished even veterans. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Today the old mine yard is quiet, the headframe long since dismantled for salvage, but the outline of the ore bins and the concrete footings of the hoist room are still legible to a practiced eye. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Baraga County Iron Mine
mines.description: When metallurgical improvements made previously uneconomic ore grades profitable in 1877, the Baraga County Iron Mine was organized to work a deposit that had been known but untouched for a decade. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. A series of rich bonanza pockets in the middle levels sustained production through a period when the main ore body was running lean, buying time for exploration that eventually found new ore. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. The shaft was sunk in stages, each new level revealing ore continuity that justified the next round of capital expenditure and kept investors engaged through the inevitable slow periods. The mine grew steadily through its first decade, reaching a workforce of nearly a hundred men and a ten-head stamp mill capable of treating several hundred tons of ore per month. Preservation efforts have protected the most significant surface structures; the mine is listed on state and national historic registers and continues to attract researchers studying the industrial archaeology of western mining. Metal detecting requires a free permit from the local ranger district before visiting — contact the National Forest supervisor's office to obtain one.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Brule Mine (Gogebic)
mines.description: A retired mining engineer purchased the unproven ground in 1890 and organized the Brule Mine (Gogebic) after his own sampling program revealed values that the original prospectors had overlooked. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. The property changed hands twice during its productive years, each transfer bringing fresh capital and a new management approach that extended productive life beyond what the original operators had envisioned. Family men who planned to stay rather than drift formed the backbone of the camp's permanent population, building small farms on nearby land and sending their children to the district school. A stamp mill built a quarter-mile from the portal processed the crushed ore, its rhythmic thudding audible across the valley floor during peak production. The property fell in the comfortable middle of the district's size spectrum — large enough to justify professional management and a proper mill, small enough to remain nimble during price downturns. Aerial photographs reveal the full extent of the underground workings through surface subsidence patterns — shallow depressions that trace the main vein system for nearly half a mile across the hillside. Metal detecting is restricted on National Forest land; visitors must obtain written authorization from the ranger district before bringing detectors onto this property.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - USFS Ottawa National Forest

mines.name: Tecumseh Mine (Keweenaw)
mines.description: The Tecumseh Mine (Keweenaw) was staked in 1859 when a prospector following a stringer of chalcopyrite traced it uphill to a massive sulfide body that would anchor the district's copper economy for decades. Copper values ran consistently throughout the deposit, and the absence of dramatic bonanza grades was compensated by the sheer volume of ore available for extraction. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The head frame was a landmark for miles around, its silhouette visible from the valley road and serving as a symbol of the camp's industrial ambition. A polyglot workforce gave the camp its distinctive character: Serbian and Croatian miners who had come from the copper mines of Central Europe occupied one end of camp, while Mexican miners who had followed silver veins north formed a tight-knit community at the other. Fire destroyed the mill building in the mine's eleventh year; rather than rebuild the stamp mill, the company erected a modern concentrating plant that improved metal recovery significantly. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Visitors interested in metal detecting should confirm land status and regulations with the relevant land management agency before visiting.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - Keweenaw National Historical Park

mines.name: Copper Harbor Mine (Fort Wilkins)
mines.description: The Copper Harbor Mine (Fort Wilkins) dates to 1844, when surface copper carbonates — bright green and unmistakable against the gray country rock — led a prospecting party to stake claims on what proved to be a substantial porphyry deposit. The ore carried significant gold and silver credits alongside copper, which improved smelter returns and allowed profitable operation at copper prices that would have shut down a less complex deposit. Ore shipments were made by pack mule in the earliest years, the long train of animals winding down the mountain road to the nearest freight depot twice a week. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. A Methodist church built by mine families in the camp's early years provided a counterpoint to the saloon culture, and its congregation was a force for stability in an otherwise transient population. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. Preservation efforts have protected the most significant surface structures; the mine is listed on state and national historic registers and continues to attract researchers studying the industrial archaeology of western mining. This site falls under state park jurisdiction where metal detecting and artifact removal are strictly prohibited.

mines.name: Snowslide Gulch Mine
mines.description: The Snowslide Gulch Mine was staked in 1891 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. Multiple interconnected stopes worked simultaneously allowed the mine to maintain steady production even when one working face hit barren ground or required re-timbering. Water was a constant adversary; the lower levels required steam-driven pumps running around the clock to keep the workings dry enough for drilling and blasting. Labor relations were tense through much of the mine's life; the Western Federation of Miners organized the workforce in the early twentieth century, and a series of strikes tested management's resolve. Ventilation was improved each time the mine deepened, with raise connections linking levels and powered fans installed at the portal to maintain a steady air circulation through the working stopes. A well-organized medium-scale operation, the mine maintained consistent production over a long period rather than the dramatic boom-and-bust cycles that characterized many neighboring properties. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Snowslide Gulch Mine site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.

mines.name: Treadwell Extension Mine
mines.description: The Treadwell Extension Mine was among the first deep mines in the district, sinking past the 1889 surface placers to reach the primary lode that would prove its real value. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. The mine's electrical system — installed after the power company ran a line through the district — replaced steam with electric hoists, fans, and pumps, dramatically cutting operating costs. An aerial tram carried ore buckets over a steep ridge to a mill in the adjacent valley, a practical solution to terrain that would have made surface haulage prohibitively expensive. The mine's Chinese workers, hired for surface and timbering tasks at wages below those of white miners, formed a self-contained community at the edge of camp and maintained ties to a Chinese-operated store in the nearest town. Powder men carefully drilled and loaded shot holes at the end of each shift, timing blasts for the change-over period when the workings were clear of men. The operation ranked among the district's largest, with a workforce exceeding two hundred in peak years and a mill complex capable of processing hundreds of tons of ore per day. The ruins of the mill foundation, the flat ribbon of gray tailings, and the gated collar of the main shaft are all that remain of a property that once employed hundreds. Metal detecting is permitted on this publicly managed parcel; the tailings and surrounding ground are open to recreational prospectors under BLM guidelines.

mines.name: Fisher Mine (Juneau)
mines.description: In the second year of the big rush, 1904, the Fisher Mine (Juneau) differentiated itself from dozens of flash-in-the-pan ventures by hitting a thick, continuous ore body at only forty feet of depth. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. Prospectors had worked this ground seasonally for years before organized capital arrived; their shallow shafts and test pits dotted the hillside and guided systematic deep development. Management installed a narrow-gauge tramway from the portal to the mill, replacing the mule-drawn ore wagons that had struggled over the rough canyon road. A small town grew up around the mine: a post office, a two-room schoolhouse, three saloons, and the inevitable assay office that doubled as banker and source of local credit. Flooding in the lower workings following an unusually wet spring required emergency pumping operations that ran for six weeks before the water level was brought back under control. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Decades after final closure, the mine's footprint — shafts, adits, tailings, mill site — remains one of the most prominent physical features in this part of the district. Metal detecting is allowed with a free-use permit from the ranger district; contact the National Forest office before planning a detecting outing here.

mines.name: Joe Juneau Placer Discovery
mines.description: The Joe Juneau Placer Discovery was incorporated in 1880 following a season of intensive surface sampling that convinced its organizers the deposit was large enough to justify a major capital investment. The stamp mill processed several hundred tons of ore per day at peak output, recovering gold through amalgamation and later through a Wilfley table circuit for fine-gold capture. The ore was divided into three grades at the sorting house: rich ore for direct smelter shipment, mill ore for concentration, and waste rock for the dump — a careful sorting regime that maximized recovery. Rock from the upper levels was so competent that heavy timber sets were not required, keeping support costs low and allowing faster advance in the critical early development phase. Veterans of the Comstock, the California camps, and the Colorado silver fields found their way here, contributing deep practical knowledge to a management that was often long on capital and short on mining experience. The discovery of a second ore body, separated from the main vein by a barren porphyry dike, was made by an experienced shifter who noticed a change in wall-rock alteration that others had dismissed. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. Environmental remediation has cleaned up the worst contamination from tailings runoff, but the site retains enough industrial character to clearly communicate its mining heritage. Metal detecting is permitted on this publicly managed parcel; the tailings and surrounding ground are open to recreational prospectors under BLM guidelines.

mines.name: Star Creek Mine (Circle)
mines.description: Organized in 1894 by a stock company formed in the state capital, the Star Creek Mine (Circle) quickly justified its investors' faith when the first deep level revealed ore far richer than the near-surface sampling had suggested. The footwall side of the vein consistently ran higher in gold than the hanging wall, and experienced miners learned to work the ore selectively to maximize recovery and minimize dilution. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. The mine's machine shop could fabricate most replacement parts on-site, reducing the costly delays that plagued more remote operations dependent on long supply chains. At the peak of production the camp held several hundred residents; today only stone foundations and a few rotting timbers remain to suggest the scale of the activity. A runaway ore train on the inclined haulage wrecked the ore bin and damaged the crusher, shutting down production for three weeks while repairs were made and the haulage brake system redesigned. Employment peaked at around eighty men during the best years, with a ten-stamp mill and a complete surface plant that included a machine shop and carpenter shed. The region's mining heritage tourism industry has found the Star Creek Mine (Circle) site to be one of its most evocative stops, combining accessible ruins with a well-documented historical record. Metal detecting is generally permitted on this BLM-administered parcel; casual hobbyist use is allowed under the guiding regulations, though removing significant artifacts or disturbing the historic structures requires a permit.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - BLM Alaska Circle District

mines.name: Crooked Creek Mine (Circle)
mines.description: A government geologist mapping the district in 1896 noted the gold-bearing structure in his survey report; within months, private capital had organized the Crooked Creek Mine (Circle) and begun sinking. Free gold could be seen with the naked eye in the richest stopes, and careful mucking by experienced miners salvaged small pockets that would have been lost in a less attentive operation. The ore was hoisted in iron skips guided by timber sets in the shaft, then dumped into ore bins from which it was fed to the crusher and then the mill. Company housing lined a terrace above the mill; the rows of identical frame houses, company store, and assay office gave the camp the look of a planned industrial town. The post office, which served the camp and surrounding district for three decades, was the connection to the wider world; its records give historians one of their most reliable windows into the composition of the workforce. The assay office ran continuous checks on every ore car, allowing the sorting crew to segregate high-grade ore for direct smelter shipment from the lower-grade mill feed. At peak operation the mine employed around sixty men and ran a ten-stamp mill that processed ore seven days a week during the productive seasons. The mill foundations, settling ponds, and rusting machinery form an open-air industrial museum that attracts photographers and history enthusiasts. Metal detecting is allowed on this BLM parcel; prospectors frequently visit the tailings and surrounding ground, though no active digging into historic structures is permitted without a cultural-resource clearance.
mines.source: USGS MRDS - BLM Alaska Circle District

mines.name: American Creek Mine (Circle)
mines.description: The American Creek Mine (Circle) was staked in 1897 by a consortium of seasoned drifters who pooled their last funds to sink a test shaft — and struck the vein that justified every cent. A second parallel vein found in the hanging wall at the third level transformed the mine's economics, providing enough ore to run two stamp-mill heads simultaneously.
